title: 2026/03/12 - PP
displayTitle: 2026-03-11 - Primary Production Committee - Petition of Ian Sizer: Madagascar Ragwort – A growing concern for Northland and the rest of New Zealand
topic: Petition of Ian Sizer: Madagascar Ragwort – A growing concern for Northland and the rest of New Zealand
description: Thursday, 12 March 2026 | Primary Production Select Committee Hearing of evidence: Petition of Ian Sizer: Madagascar Ragwort – A growing concern for Northland and the rest of New Zealand Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Simon Upton, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Dr James Newman, Senior Advisor Science Dr Anna Hooper, Principal Advisor – Programmes Geoff Simmons, Chief Advisor – Policy and Strategy Briefing on the PCE report Reviewing the use of the Land Use Capability system in a regulatory context Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Simon Upton, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Dr James Newman, Senior Advisor Science Dr Anna Hooper, Principal Advisor – Programmes Geoff Simmons, Chief Advisor – Policy and Strategy Nora Lanari, Senior Advisor Research and Analysis
descriptionSummary: Petition of Ian Sizer: Madagascar Ragwort – A growing concern for Northland and the rest of New Zealand. Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment. Simon Upton, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment

So I'm gonna be really brief and cover a few general and specific points. Ecologist talk about invasion curves and point out how management options change depending on how widespread and established a particular pest is.

So actual eradication is typically only an option when things are just getting going within a specified area. There is no record in New Zealand of any terrestrial plant having been successfully eradicated when the extent of spread has been greater than one hectare. That's quite a sobering point to, to sort of sink in. So eradication is, is not really an option for this species, but maybe some subnational areas might be kept free of the rag war.

But trials here indicate that this approach is much, much less effective.

As you would have left heard already from a research on our BSI, uh, that need for a distribution map has been raised several times, but there are several problems. More so that even photos of plants are sometimes not good enough to differentiate between the two. And what this means that there might be areas where, uh, no observations of Madagascar have been reported, but it might be the, the case of not that weed being truly absent from those areas, but more of the fact that no one went searching for this swed in the areas.

So given these challenges, but against the backdrop of really the need for a distribution map, uh, you could consider approaching again, NPI and North on Regional Council and asking them for a better distribution map. Not just those publicly available publicly recorded observations, but more broadly that adequate information on mad gas redwood should be part of the good data system and what a good data system looks like in the context of broader we management has been described in the space in WAY report, but in the context of, uh,

monitoring the spread of Madagascar Redwood, uh, four things are key. So first is robust data on with location, and that again includes the locations where the weed has been looked for and found, and locations where the we has been looked for but has not been found.

Second is the data on the stage of invasion. Essentially that means knowing how widespread is the weed currently, how much further it can spread, and what habitats are likely to be suitable. And from what we know to date from the modeling time, the vast majority of New Zealand habitats, especially in the North Island, are considered favorable or suitable for Madagascar Agri War.

That map is in the research report, but the third, the data on spread over time. In summary, you really need the data on the current location and the rate of spread over time and a good robust, uh, distribution map could be a good place to start. Um, look, I'd, I'd like to take you through the findings of, um, my, one of my latest reports, which is reviewing the use of the LUC system in a regulatory context.

And that was published in December and I've already had a chance to brief the environment committee on this. Uh, and I'm delighted that, that I think we actually, we were trying to do it jointly and, and I was there. You were there, okay. Yeah. Yeah, I was there. So I'm gonna have a second, second crack. Okay. It was that good. Was it? It was, there was, um, I think they were trying to get a joint meeting and it, we couldn't get the scheduling.

Yeah. So that's why I'm here separately. Uh, let me start by outlining what the land use capability, uh, system is. LUC stands for Land Use Capability. It's a land classification system, which was developed in the 1950s, and it was designed to support long-term sustained production on the land.

Uh, LEC as many of you know, is made out of eight classes, one to eight. And within each of the classes, there are subclasses based on the dominant limitation of productivity. So it's only the dominant limitation. There are four limitations. There is, uh, erod ability, we soil and climate. And then within the sub classes there are also units.

So it's got a certain level of disaggregation, however, it was never designed to address environmental s So the first question you might ask is, why review the use of the LUC system? Well, for a biological economy like ours, high quality information about the land and wider landscape is critical.

And it's also critical for high quality environmental regulation. Now, currently, the LUC systems being re relied on to support three pieces of national regulation. They are the policy statement for highly productive land, the national environmental standard for commercial forestry and the Climate Change Response Act to limit ETS driven Farm to forestry conversion.

So those are the three current, uh, regulatory uses of the system. It, uc is also used in two regional regulatory plans in the context of water quality and nutrient management. Given that it wasn't designed to address environmental management challenges, but is now being used to support regulation, I thought it was prudent to review whether the system was sufficiently robust to support regulation and particularly so given that Parliament has a major new planning system in front of it, in front of the environment committee

with these two bills planning and natural environment. So the key finding of my review is that the LUC system is ill suited to regulatory use. This is due to several limitations. The first one is that it's outdated. This is because the main mapping was carried out about half a century ago in the 1970s and eighties.

Not surprisingly, uh, things have moved since then. That mapping remains unchanged. It doesn't reflect the situation today. For example, land management practices such as irrigation and drainage directly affect the subclass limitation of witness.

The addition of permanent irrigation can change the LUC unit as the potential created by irrigation wasn't considered during the original assessment in the 1970s and 1980s Kennedy Plains. Um, it's a good example of that at the time of mapping manual technologies and a high degree of expert judgment were used.

Now that was unavoidable, but it introduced a high degree of subjectivity, particularly into the boundaries between areas. And it's not a criticism. I mean, they did the very best job they could at the time. It's just that we've moved on and we've got new techniques for doing stuff.

Second limitation is the course scale of the mapping ma, which makes the the national LUC data set inappropriate for use at a property scale. And this is where most of us who own land have immediately rushed to look at what these lines mean. And you know, your own farm inside out, and then you look at what the line produces and there seems to be a gap.

Well, that's scarcity surprising because the scale of the LUC data set is one 50,000. So every centimeter on a map is 500 meters on the ground. But to make operational or site-based decisions, you need maps at the scale of one to 5,000 or one to 10,000.

So that would be a centimeter on the map equaling 50 or uh, a hundred meters. Um, it's as you, as some of you will know anyhow, that the, the land can change much more frequently than at a 500 meter interval. I, I can't resist telling you I've, I missed snow from my own farm paddocks, which move from being well drained to not so well draining.

It happens much more, uh, much more swiftly and much more irregularly than the maps tell you. This is all out washed from the central north island volcanoes.

But it's quite fascinating. But what, what she's doing is very, very fine grained what's happening on the surface doesn't follow the course conclusions of LUC. And that would be true everywhere. It's not a criticism of LDC being done, you know that it's what they could do at the time. But we're, we're moving beyond there.

Now let me just outline how these limitations play out in national regulations. And I'll start with highly productive land. Let's look at the tangible example from the Hawke's Bay where the council explored different methods to identify highly productive land. Now the pink colors, and you need to watch these slides just to understand this, the pink colors on this map, this is a bit of the Hawkes Bay.

You can see Hastings towards the top there. Um, the pink colors show areas which are classed as one to three under the LUC system. So, uh, that's the rest of it is outside that. Now the yellow colors on the right hand map show the class one to three land that show up when you are using an alternative map.

This is using smap, which is the digital soil map. Okay? And as you could probably just see even with your eyes, they don't match neatly. Now the teal colored areas show the overlap between the two and the overlap is just 41%. So if you used one map, you'd get one set of results.

If you'd used another technique, sap, you get another. And the LUC only overlaps with the more sophisticated smap 41% of the time. The rest doesn't. That's a very significant disagreement between the dataset. Similar analysis in Canterbury showed even lower agreement of around 21%.

Now, the point is that you would need substantial ground truthing to reconcile and validate the two. You would be ill-advised to solely rely on either of the methods for identification of highly productive land. So in practical terms, if, if you were concerned that being classed as highly productive could limit your land use options, the lack of overlap could spill real problems for you.

These are real regulatory problems caused by inadequate underlying data. Just look at the pink bits south of Hastings. Um, the digital soil map doesn't give these a high status, but the LUC map does that cuts two ways depending on which side of the coin you're on as to what you do with the land.

But, but it, it is a very significant difference. Okay. The second illustration I've chosen of how this LUC system plays out is in respect of the, uh, national environmental standard on commercial forestry. Uh, the, that standard has been, again, derived from the LUC system, um, using something called an erosion susceptibility classification as a screening tool.

Uh, and that identifies the erosion susceptibility of land intended for forestry. So it's a threshold test used to determine whether a forest activity is permitted or subject to certain conditions being met or whether it requires a resource consent outright. So given that erosion from forestry activities is a key environmental issue that the, the standard is supposed to manage, identifying risky land is critical.

That relies on the rebut robustness of the erosion classification and the national, uh, erosion susceptibility classification data set, which as I say is based on, uh, LUC, unfortunately, the ESC both over and under classifies susceptibility of land to erosion, obviously under classifying land as a problem environmentally because it gives the green light to forestry when maybe more questions should be asked and stricter controls imposed.

And you can see what people up in Tarara feel about that. This just explore, um, an example again from the Hawke's Bay. So this, uh, map, uh, no, I'm gonna go back. Um, so I don't distract you. But there, um, but we, this is something I think for a, for a land-based economy in a country like New Zealand, we should have this absolutely comprehensively. So we, we can't always stitch information together in the way that we've managed to do here for you. There's bits of New Zealand where you couldn't do that. So that brings me to, uh, my recommendations first that as a result of the limitations I've outlined, we shouldn't be using LUC for any more regulatory purposes.
